Geometry

Cross Product

Consider the vectors A ¯ A ¯ bar(A)\bar{A}A¯ and B ¯ B ¯ bar(B)\bar{B}B¯ as shown above the cross by
A ¯ × B ¯ = | x ^ y ^ z ^ a x a y 0 b 0 0 | = − a y b z ^ A ¯ × B ¯ = x ^ y ^ z ^ a x a y 0 b 0 0 = − a y b z ^ {:[ bar(A)xx bar(B)=|[ hat(x), hat(y), hat(z)],[a_(x),a_(y),0],[b,0,0]|],[=-a_(y)b hat(z)]:}\begin{aligned} \bar{A} \times \bar{B} & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{x} & \hat{y} & \hat{z} \\ a_{x} & a_{y} & 0 \\ b & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right| \\ & =-a_{y} b \hat{z} \end{aligned}A¯×B¯=|x^y^z^axay0b00|=−aybz^
the arec of the parallelogram
defined by A ¯ A ¯ bar(A)\bar{A}A¯ and B ¯ B ¯ bar(B)\bar{B}B¯ is | A ¯ | | B ¯ | sin ⁡ θ | A ¯ | | B ¯ | sin ⁡ θ | bar(A)|| bar(B)|sin theta|\bar{A}||\bar{B}| \sin \theta|A¯||B¯|sin⁡θ
Now
| A ¯ | sin ⁡ θ = a y | B ¯ | = b | A ¯ | sin ⁡ θ = a y | B ¯ | = b {:[| bar(A)|sin theta=a_(y)],[| bar(B)|=b]:}\begin{aligned} & |\bar{A}| \sin \theta=a_{y} \\ & |\bar{B}|=b \end{aligned}|A¯|sin⁡θ=ay|B¯|=b
tms
| A ¯ × B ¯ | = | A → | | B ¯ | sin ⁡ θ | A ¯ × B ¯ | = | A → | | B ¯ | sin ⁡ θ | bar(A)xx bar(B)|=| vec(A)|| bar(B)|sin theta|\bar{A} \times \bar{B}|=|\vec{A}||\bar{B}| \sin \theta|A¯×B¯|=|A→||B¯|sin⁡θ
Conster the more general case
A ¯ × B ¯ = | x ^ y ^ z ^ a x a y 0 b x b y 0 | = ( a x b y − b x a y ) z ^ A ¯ × B ¯ = x ^ y ^ z ^ a x a y 0 b x b y 0 = a x b y − b x a y z ^ {:[ bar(A)xx bar(B)=|[ hat(x), hat(y), hat(z)],[a_(x),a_(y),0],[b_(x),b_(y),0]|],[quad=(a_(x)b_(y)-b_(x)a_(y)) hat(z)]:}\begin{array}{r} \bar{A} \times \bar{B}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} \hat{x} & \hat{y} & \hat{z} \\ a_{x} & a_{y} & 0 \\ b_{x} & b_{y} & 0 \end{array}\right| \\ \quad=\left(a_{x} b_{y}-b_{x} a_{y}\right) \hat{z} \end{array}A¯×B¯=|x^y^z^axay0bxby0|=(axby−bxay)z^
Just as in the previous case the area of the parcillelogram
defined by A A AAA and is gluen
by
| A ¯ | | B ¯ | sin ⁡ θ | A ¯ | | B ¯ | sin ⁡ θ | bar(A)|| bar(B)|sin theta|\bar{A}||\bar{B}| \sin \theta|A¯||B¯|sin⁡θ
Now
a x = | A ¯ | cos ⁡ ( θ + γ ) a y = | A ¯ | sin ⁡ ( θ + γ ) b x = | B ¯ | cos ⁡ γ b y = | B ¯ | sin ⁡ γ | A ¯ × B ¯ | = | a x b y − b y a y | a x = | A ¯ | cos ⁡ ( θ + γ ) a y = | A ¯ | sin ⁡ ( θ + γ ) b x = | B ¯ | cos ⁡ γ b y = | B ¯ | sin ⁡ γ | A ¯ × B ¯ | = a x b y − b y a y {:[a_(x)=| bar(A)|cos(theta+gamma)],[a_(y)=| bar(A)|sin(theta+gamma)],[b_(x)=| bar(B)|cos gamma],[b_(y)=| bar(B)|sin gamma],[| bar(A)xx bar(B)|=|a_(x)b_(y)-b_(y)a_(y)|]:}\begin{aligned} a_{x} & =|\bar{A}| \cos (\theta+\gamma) \\ a_{y} & =|\bar{A}| \sin (\theta+\gamma) \\ b_{x} & =|\bar{B}| \cos \gamma \\ b_{y} & =|\bar{B}| \sin \gamma \\ |\bar{A} \times \bar{B}| & =\left|a_{x} b_{y}-b_{y} a_{y}\right| \end{aligned}ax=|A¯|cos⁡(θ+γ)ay=|A¯|sin⁡(θ+γ)bx=|B¯|cos⁡γby=|B¯|sin⁡γ|A¯×B¯|=|axby−byay|
= | A ¯ | | B ¯ | | cos ⁡ ( θ − γ ) sin ⁡ γ − sin ⁡ ( θ − γ ) cos ⁡ γ | = | A ¯ | | B ¯ | | cos ⁡ ( θ − γ ) sin ⁡ γ − sin ⁡ ( θ − γ ) cos ⁡ γ | =| bar(A)|| bar(B)||cos(theta-gamma)sin gamma-sin(theta-gamma)cos gamma|=|\bar{A}||\bar{B}||\cos (\theta-\gamma) \sin \gamma-\sin (\theta-\gamma) \cos \gamma|=|A¯||B¯||cos⁡(θ−γ)sin⁡γ−sin⁡(θ−γ)cos⁡γ|
But
sin ⁡ ( θ + γ ) = sin ⁡ θ cos ⁡ γ + cos ⁡ θ sin ⁡ γ cos ⁡ ( θ + γ ) = cos ⁡ θ cos ⁡ γ − sin ⁡ θ sin ⁡ γ sin ⁡ ( θ + γ ) = sin ⁡ θ cos ⁡ γ + cos ⁡ θ sin ⁡ γ cos ⁡ ( θ + γ ) = cos ⁡ θ cos ⁡ γ − sin ⁡ θ sin ⁡ γ {:[sin(theta+gamma)=sin theta cos gamma+cos theta sin gamma],[cos(theta+gamma)=cos theta cos gamma-sin theta sin gamma]:}\begin{aligned} & \sin (\theta+\gamma)=\sin \theta \cos \gamma+\cos \theta \sin \gamma \\ & \cos (\theta+\gamma)=\cos \theta \cos \gamma-\sin \theta \sin \gamma \end{aligned}sin⁡(θ+γ)=sin⁡θcos⁡γ+cos⁡θsin⁡γcos⁡(θ+γ)=cos⁡θcos⁡γ−sin⁡θsin⁡γ
and
cos ⁡ ( θ − γ ) sin ⁡ γ = sin ⁡ γ cos ⁡ θ cos ⁡ γ − sin ⁡ θ sin 2 ⁡ γ sin ⁡ ( θ + γ ) cos ⁡ γ = sin ⁡ θ cos 2 ⁡ γ + cos ⁡ θ sin ⁡ γ cos ⁡ γ cos ⁡ ( θ − γ ) sin ⁡ γ = sin ⁡ γ cos ⁡ θ cos ⁡ γ − sin ⁡ θ sin 2 ⁡ γ sin ⁡ ( θ + γ ) cos ⁡ γ = sin ⁡ θ cos 2 ⁡ γ + cos ⁡ θ sin ⁡ γ cos ⁡ γ {:[cos(theta-gamma)sin gamma=sin gamma cos theta cos gamma],[-sin thetasin^(2)gamma],[sin(theta+gamma)cos gamma=sin thetacos^(2)gamma],[+cos theta sin gamma cos gamma]:}\begin{aligned} & \cos (\theta-\gamma) \sin \gamma=\sin \gamma \cos \theta \cos \gamma \\ & -\sin \theta \sin ^{2} \gamma \\ & \sin (\theta+\gamma) \cos \gamma=\sin \theta \cos ^{2} \gamma \\ & +\cos \theta \sin \gamma \cos \gamma \end{aligned}cos⁡(θ−γ)sin⁡γ=sin⁡γcos⁡θcos⁡γ−sin⁡θsin2⁡γsin⁡(θ+γ)cos⁡γ=sin⁡θcos2⁡γ+cos⁡θsin⁡γcos⁡γ
If follows that
cos ⁡ ( θ + γ ) sin ⁡ γ − sin ⁡ ( θ + γ ) cos ⁡ γ = cos ⁡ ( θ + γ ) sin ⁡ γ − sin ⁡ ( θ + γ ) cos ⁡ γ = cos(theta+gamma)sin gamma-sin(theta+gamma)cos gamma=\cos (\theta+\gamma) \sin \gamma-\sin (\theta+\gamma) \cos \gamma=cos⁡(θ+γ)sin⁡γ−sin⁡(θ+γ)cos⁡γ=
sin ⁡ γ cos ⁡ θ cos ⁡ γ − sin ⁡ θ sin 2 ⁡ γ − sin ⁡ θ cos 2 ⁡ γ sin ⁡ γ cos ⁡ θ cos ⁡ γ − sin ⁡ θ sin 2 ⁡ γ − sin ⁡ θ cos 2 ⁡ γ sin gamma cos theta cos gamma-sin thetasin^(2)gamma-sin thetacos^(2)gamma\sin \gamma \cos \theta \cos \gamma-\sin \theta \sin ^{2} \gamma-\sin \theta \cos ^{2} \gammasin⁡γcos⁡θcos⁡γ−sin⁡θsin2⁡γ−sin⁡θcos2⁡γ
− cos ⁡ θ / sin ⁡ γ cos ⁡ γ = − ( sin ⁡ θ sin 2 ⁡ γ + sin θ ⁡ θ cos 2 ⁡ γ ) = − sin ⁡ θ ( sin 2 ⁡ γ + cos 2 ⁡ γ ) = − sin ⁡ θ − cos ⁡ θ / sin ⁡ γ cos ⁡ γ = − sin ⁡ θ sin 2 ⁡ γ + sin θ ⁡ θ cos 2 ⁡ γ = − sin ⁡ θ sin 2 ⁡ γ + cos 2 ⁡ γ = − sin ⁡ θ {:[-cos theta//sin gamma cos gamma],[=-(sin thetasin^(2)gamma+sin^(theta)thetacos^(2)gamma)],[=-sin theta(sin^(2)gamma+cos^(2)gamma)=-sin theta]:}\begin{aligned} & -\cos \theta / \sin \gamma \cos \gamma \\ = & -\left(\sin \theta \sin ^{2} \gamma+\sin ^{\theta} \theta \cos ^{2} \gamma\right) \\ = & -\sin \theta\left(\sin ^{2} \gamma+\cos ^{2} \gamma\right)=-\sin \theta \end{aligned}−cos⁡θ/sin⁡γcos⁡γ=−(sin⁡θsin2⁡γ+sinθ⁡θcos2⁡γ)=−sin⁡θ(sin2⁡γ+cos2⁡γ)=−sin⁡θ
so
| A ¯ × B ¯ | = | A ¯ | | B ¯ | 1 − sin ⁡ θ ∣ = | A ¯ | | B ¯ | sin ⁡ θ | A ¯ × B ¯ | = | A ¯ | | B ¯ | 1 − sin ⁡ θ ∣ = | A ¯ | | B ¯ | sin ⁡ θ {:[| bar(A)xx bar(B)|=| bar(A)|| bar(B)|1-sin theta∣],[=| bar(A)|| bar(B)|sin theta]:}\begin{aligned} |\bar{A} \times \bar{B}| & =|\bar{A}||\bar{B}| 1-\sin \theta \mid \\ & =|\bar{A}||\bar{B}| \sin \theta \end{aligned}|A¯×B¯|=|A¯||B¯|1−sin⁡θ∣=|A¯||B¯|sin⁡θ
which is the grea of the
parallelogram defined by A A AAA and
B.

Jacobian in Two Dimensions

In one dimension a transformation the form
x = g ( y ) x = g ( y ) x=g(y)x=g(y)x=g(y)
applied to the integral
∫ a b f ( x ) d x ∫ a b   f ( x ) d x int_(a)^(b)f(x)dx\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x∫abf(x)dx
gives
d x = d g d y d y d x = d g d y d y dx=(dg)/(dy)dyd x=\frac{d g}{d y} d ydx=dgdydy
and
∫ a b f ( x ) d x = ∫ g − 1 ( a ) g ( b ) f ( g ( y ) ) d g d y d y ∫ a b   f ( x ) d x = ∫ g − 1 ( a ) g ( b )   f ( g ( y ) ) d g d y d y int_(a)^(b)f(x)dx=int_(g^(-1)(a))^(g(b))f(g(y))(dg)/(dy)dy\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x=\int_{g^{-1}(a)}^{g(b)} f(g(y)) \frac{d g}{d y} d y∫abf(x)dx=∫g−1(a)g(b)f(g(y))dgdydy
In two dimesions the transformation complex.
Consider the transforwation
x 1 = g 1 ( y 1 , y 2 ) x 2 = g 2 ( y 1 , y 2 ) x 1 = g 1 y 1 , y 2 x 2 = g 2 y 1 , y 2 {:[x_(1)=g_(1)(y_(1),y_(2))],[x_(2)=g_(2)(y_(1),y_(2))]:}\begin{aligned} & x_{1}=g_{1}\left(y_{1}, y_{2}\right) \\ & x_{2}=g_{2}\left(y_{1}, y_{2}\right) \end{aligned}x1=g1(y1,y2)x2=g2(y1,y2)
and the integral
∬ R f ( x 1 , x 2 ) d x 1 d x 2 ∬ R   f x 1 , x 2 d x 1 d x 2 ∬_(R)f(x_(1),x_(2))dx_(1)dx_(2)\iint_{R} f\left(x_{1}, x_{2}\right) d x_{1} d x_{2}∬Rf(x1,x2)dx1dx2
where
Now consider the transform of the ( y 1 ) 1 y 2 y 1 1 y 2 (y_(1))_(1)y_(2)\left(y_{1}\right)_{1} y_{2}(y1)1y2 coordinale system into the ( x 1 , x 2 ) x 1 , x 2 (x_(1),x_(2))\left(x_{1}, x_{2}\right)(x1,x2) coordirate system
In the ( x 1 , x 2 ) x 1 , x 2 (x_(1),x_(2))\left(x_{1}, x_{2}\right)(x1,x2) coordinates
d x 1 = ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1 d y 1 i ^ + ∂ g 2 ∂ y 1 d y 1 j ^ d x 2 = ∂ g 1 ∂ y 2 d y 2 i ^ + ∂ g 2 ∂ y 2 d y 2 j ^ d x 1 = ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1 d y 1 i ^ + ∂ g 2 ∂ y 1 d y 1 j ^ d x 2 = ∂ g 1 ∂ y 2 d y 2 i ^ + ∂ g 2 ∂ y 2 d y 2 j ^ {:[dx_(1)=(delg_(1))/(dely_(1))dy_(1) hat(i)+(delg_(2))/(dely_(1))dy_(1) hat(j)],[dx_(2)=(delg_(1))/(dely_(2))dy_(2) hat(i)+(delg_(2))/(dely_(2))dy_(2) hat(j)]:}\begin{aligned} & d x_{1}=\frac{\partial g_{1}}{\partial y_{1}} d y_{1} \hat{i}+\frac{\partial g_{2}}{\partial y_{1}} d y_{1} \hat{j} \\ & d x_{2}=\frac{\partial g_{1}}{\partial y_{2}} d y_{2} \hat{i}+\frac{\partial g_{2}}{\partial y_{2}} d y_{2} \hat{j} \end{aligned}dx1=∂g1∂y1dy1i^+∂g2∂y1dy1j^dx2=∂g1∂y2dy2i^+∂g2∂y2dy2j^
So C A → = J x 1 × d x ¯ 2 = | ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1 h 1 ∂ g 2 d y 1 ∂ y 1 0 ∂ g 1 h 2 ∂ y 2 ∂ g 2 h k ∂ y 2 0 | = | ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1 h 1 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 1 y 1 ∂ g 1 ∂ y 2 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 2 h k | k = ( ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 2 d 1 d y 2 − ∂ g 2 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 1 ∂ y 2 d y 1 d y 2 ) k ^ = ( ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 2 − ∂ g 2 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 1 ∂ y 2 ) d y 1 d y 2 k ^ = | ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 1 ∂ y 2 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 2 | d y 1 d y 2 k ^  So  C A → = J x 1 × d x ¯ 2 = ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1 h 1 ∂ g 2 d y 1 ∂ y 1 0 ∂ g 1 h 2 ∂ y 2 ∂ g 2 h k ∂ y 2 0 = ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1 h 1 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 1 y 1 ∂ g 1 ∂ y 2 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 2 h k k = ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 2 d 1 d y 2 − ∂ g 2 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 1 ∂ y 2 d y 1 d y 2 k ^ = ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 2 − ∂ g 2 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 1 ∂ y 2 d y 1 d y 2 k ^ = ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 1 ∂ y 2 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 2 d y 1 d y 2 k ^ {:[" So "],[ vec(CA)=J_(x_(1))xx d bar(x)_(2)=|[(delg_(1))/(dely_(1))h_(1),(delg_(2)dy_(1))/(dely_(1)),0],[(delg_(1)h_(2))/(dely_(2)),(delg_(2)h_(k))/(dely_(2)),0]|],[=|[(delg_(1))/(dely_(1))h_(1),(delg_(2))/(dely_(1))y_(1)],[(delg_(1))/(dely_(2)),(delg_(2))/(dely_(2))h_(k)]|_(k)],[=((delg_(1))/(dely_(1))(delg_(2))/(dely_(2))d_(1)dy_(2)-(delg_(2))/(dely_(1))(delg_(1))/(dely_(2))dy_(1)dy_(2)) hat(k)],[=((delg_(1))/(dely_(1))(delg_(2))/(dely_(2))-(delg_(2))/(dely_(1))(delg_(1))/(dely_(2)))dy_(1)dy_(2) hat(k)],[=|[(delg_(1))/(dely_(1)),(delg_(1))/(dely_(2))],[(delg_(2))/(dely_(1)),(delg_(2))/(dely_(2))]|d_(y_(1))d_(y_(2)) hat(k)]:}\begin{aligned} & \text { So } \\ & \overrightarrow{C A}=J_{x_{1}} \times d \bar{x}_{2}=\left|\begin{array}{lll} \frac{\partial g_{1}}{\partial y_{1}} h_{1} & \frac{\partial g_{2} d y_{1}}{\partial y_{1}} & 0 \\ \frac{\partial g_{1} h_{2}}{\partial y_{2}} & \frac{\partial g_{2} h_{k}}{\partial y_{2}} & 0 \end{array}\right| \\ & =\left|\begin{array}{ll} \frac{\partial g_{1}}{\partial y_{1}} h_{1} & \frac{\partial g_{2}}{\partial y_{1}} y_{1} \\ \frac{\partial g_{1}}{\partial y_{2}} & \frac{\partial g_{2}}{\partial y_{2}} h_{k} \end{array}\right|_{k} \\ & =\left(\frac{\partial g_{1}}{\partial y_{1}} \frac{\partial g_{2}}{\partial y_{2}} d_{1} d y_{2}-\frac{\partial g_{2}}{\partial y_{1}} \frac{\partial g_{1}}{\partial y_{2}} d y_{1} d y_{2}\right) \hat{k} \\ & =\left(\frac{\partial g_{1}}{\partial y_{1}} \frac{\partial g_{2}}{\partial y_{2}}-\frac{\partial g_{2}}{\partial y_{1}} \frac{\partial g_{1}}{\partial y_{2}}\right) d y_{1} d y_{2} \hat{k} \\ & =\left|\begin{array}{ll} \frac{\partial g_{1}}{\partial y_{1}} & \frac{\partial g_{1}}{\partial y_{2}} \\ \frac{\partial g_{2}}{\partial y_{1}} & \frac{\partial g_{2}}{\partial y_{2}} \end{array}\right| d_{y_{1}} d_{y_{2}} \hat{k} \end{aligned} So CA→=Jx1×dx¯2=|∂g1∂y1h1∂g2dy1∂y10∂g1h2∂y2∂g2hk∂y20|=|∂g1∂y1h1∂g2∂y1y1∂g1∂y2∂g2∂y2hk|k=(∂g1∂y1∂g2∂y2d1dy2−∂g2∂y1∂g1∂y2dy1dy2)k^=(∂g1∂y1∂g2∂y2−∂g2∂y1∂g1∂y2)dy1dy2k^=|∂g1∂y1∂g1∂y2∂g2∂y1∂g2∂y2|dy1dy2k^
The 2D Jacoboan is defined by
the matrix the matrix
J = ( ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 1 ∂ y 2 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 1 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 2 ) J = ∂ g 1 ∂ y 1      ∂ g 1 ∂ y 2 ∂ g 2 ∂ y 1      ∂ g 2 ∂ y 2 J=([(delg_(1))/(dely_(1)),(delg_(1))/(dely_(2))],[(delg_(2))/(dely_(1)),(delg_(2))/(dely_(2))])J=\left(\begin{array}{ll} \frac{\partial g_{1}}{\partial y_{1}} & \frac{\partial g_{1}}{\partial y_{2}} \\ \frac{\partial g_{2}}{\partial y_{1}} & \frac{\partial g_{2}}{\partial y_{2}} \end{array}\right)J=(∂g1∂y1∂g1∂y2∂g2∂y1∂g2∂y2)
It follows that the area element is given by
d A ¯ = | J | d y 1 d y 2 k ^ d A ¯ = | J | d y 1 d y 2 k ^ d bar(A)=|J|dy_(1)dy_(2) hat(k)d \bar{A}=|J| d y_{1} d y_{2} \hat{k}dA¯=|J|dy1dy2k^
where 151 is the detominate of the Jacobian. It follows that
d A = | J | d y 1 d y 2 d A = | J | d y 1 d y 2 dA=|J|dy_(1)dy_(2)d A=|J| d y_{1} d y_{2}dA=|J|dy1dy2
and the tranformed integral is
∬ R f ( x 1 , y 2 ) d x 1 d x 2 = ∬ R ′ f ( g 1 ( y 1 ) y 2 ) , g 2 ( y 1 , y 2 ) | J | d y 1 d y 2 ∬ R   f x 1 , y 2 d x 1 d x 2 = ∬ R ′   f g 1 y 1 y 2 , g 2 y 1 , y 2 | J | d y 1 d y 2 {:[∬_(R)f(x_(1),y_(2))dx_(1)dx_(2)],[=∬_(R^('))f(g_(1)(y_(1))y_(2))","g_(2)(y_(1),y_(2))|J|dy_(1)dy_(2)]:}\begin{aligned} & \iint_{R} f\left(x_{1}, y_{2}\right) d x_{1} d x_{2} \\ = & \iint_{R^{\prime}} f\left(g_{1}\left(y_{1}\right) y_{2}\right), g_{2}\left(y_{1}, y_{2}\right)|J| d y_{1} d y_{2} \end{aligned}∬Rf(x1,y2)dx1dx2=∬R′f(g1(y1)y2),g2(y1,y2)|J|dy1dy2

Volume and Surface Area of n-Dimensional Hypersphere

In an n n nnn-dimensional Euclidean space a point is specified by the n n nnn-tuple of coordinates
( x 1 , x 2 , … , x n ) x 1 , x 2 , … , x n (x_(1),x_(2),dots,x_(n))\left(x_{1}, x_{2}, \ldots, x_{n}\right)(x1,x2,…,xn)
The set of points in the space that are included in the volume of a hypersphere of radius R R RRR must satisfy the equation
x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 ⩽ R 2 x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 ⩽ R 2 x_(1)^(2)+x_(2)^(2)+cdots+x_(n)^(2) <= R^(2)x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+\cdots+x_{n}^{2} \leqslant R^{2}x12+x22+⋯+xn2⩽R2
A volume element is defined by
d v n = d x 1 d x 2 ⋯ d x n d v n = d x 1 d x 2 ⋯ d x n dv_(n)=dx_(1)dx_(2)cdots dx_(n)d v_{n}=d x_{1} d x_{2} \cdots d x_{n}dvn=dx1dx2⋯dxn
so the volume of the sphere is given by
V n ( R ) = ∬ ⋯ ∫ d x 1 d x 2 ⋯ d x n V n ( R ) = ∬ ⋯ ∫ d x 1 d x 2 ⋯ d x n V_(n)(R)=∬cdots int dx_(1)dx_(2)cdots dx_(n)V_{n}(R)=\iint \cdots \int d x_{1} d x_{2} \cdots d x_{n}Vn(R)=∬⋯∫dx1dx2⋯dxn
V n ( R ) V n ( R ) V_(n)(R)V_{n}(R)Vn(R) must have units of R n R n R^(n)R^{n}Rn
so it is assumed that
V n ( R ) = C n R n V n ( R ) = C n R n V_(n)(R)=C_(n)R^(n)V_{n}(R)=C_{n} R^{n}Vn(R)=CnRn
This is consistent with the intuition obtamed from 1,2 and 3 dimensional spheres.
The swrface area of the sphere is defined by the equation
x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 = R 2 x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 = R 2 x_(1)^(2)+x_(2)^(2)+cdots+x_(n)^(2)=R^(2)x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+\cdots+x_{n}^{2}=R^{2}x12+x22+⋯+xn2=R2
and the integral
S n − 1 ( R ) = ∫ x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 = R 2 ∫ x 2 ⋯ d x n S n − 1 ( R ) = ∫ x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 = R 2   ∫ x 2 ⋯ d x n S_(n-1)(R)=int_(x_(1)^(2)+x_(2)^(2)+cdots+x_(n)^(2)=R^(2))intx_(2)cdots dx_(n)S_{n-1}(R)=\int_{x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+\cdots+x_{n}^{2}=R^{2}} \int x_{2} \cdots d x_{n}Sn−1(R)=∫x12+x22+⋯+xn2=R2∫x2⋯dxn
If the integral for the volume V(R) is costucted by an integral over surfaces with radius, r r rrr, where
then
O ⩽ r ⩽ R V n ( R ) = ∫ 0 R S n − 1 ( r ) d r O ⩽ r ⩽ R V n ( R ) = ∫ 0 R   S n − 1 ( r ) d r {:[O <= r <= R],[V_(n)(R)=int_(0)^(R)S_(n-1)(r)dr]:}\begin{aligned} O & \leqslant r \leqslant R \\ V_{n}(R) & =\int_{0}^{R} S_{n-1}(r) d r \end{aligned}O⩽r⩽RVn(R)=∫0RSn−1(r)dr
It follows that
S n − 1 ( R ) = d V n d R ( R ) = n C n r n − 1 S n − 1 ( R ) = d V n d R ( R ) = n C n r n − 1 S_(n-1)(R)=(dV_(n))/(dR)(R)=nC_(n)r^(n-1)S_{n-1}(R)=\frac{d V_{n}}{d R}(R)=n C_{n} r^{n-1}Sn−1(R)=dVndR(R)=nCnrn−1
Substitution into the previous integral gives
V n ( R ) = ∫ 0 R n C n r n − 1 d r = n C n ∫ 0 R r n − 1 d r = ∬ x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 ≤ R d x 2 ⋯ d x n V n ( R ) = ∫ 0 R   n C n r n − 1 d r = n C n ∫ 0 R   r n − 1 d r = ∬ x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 ≤ R   d x 2 ⋯ d x n {:[V_(n)(R)=int_(0)^(R)nC_(n)r^(n-1)dr],[=nC_(n)int_(0)^(R)r^(n-1)dr],[=∬_(x_(1)^(2)+x_(2)^(2)+cdots+x_(n)^(2) <= R)dx_(2)cdots dx_(n)]:}\begin{aligned} V_{n}(R) & =\int_{0}^{R} n C_{n} r^{n-1} d r \\ & =n C_{n} \int_{0}^{R} r^{n-1} d r \\ & =\iint_{x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+\cdots+x_{n}^{2} \leq R} d x_{2} \cdots d x_{n} \end{aligned}Vn(R)=∫0RnCnrn−1dr=nCn∫0Rrn−1dr=∬x12+x22+⋯+xn2≤Rdx2⋯dxn
These are the Cartesian and
Hyper-Spherical coordinate forms
of the integral.
volume elements satisfy
d x 1 d x 2 ⋯ d x 3 = r n − 1 d r d Ω n − 1 d x 1 d x 2 ⋯ d x 3 = r n − 1 d r d Ω n − 1 dx_(1)dx_(2)cdots dx_(3)=r^(n-1)drdOmega_(n-1)d x_{1} d x_{2} \cdots d x_{3}=r^{n-1} d r d \Omega_{n-1}dx1dx2⋯dx3=rn−1drdΩn−1
where d Ω n − 1 d Ω n − 1 dOmega_(n-1)d \Omega_{n-1}dΩn−1 is the volume contribution from the n − 1 n − 1 n-1n-1n−1 angular coordinates.
for n = 2 n = 2 n=2n=2n=2
d Ω 1 = d θ d Ω 1 = d θ dOmega_(1)=d thetad \Omega_{1}=d \thetadΩ1=dθ
and n = 3 n = 3 n=3n=3n=3
d Ω 2 = sin ⁡ θ d θ d φ d Ω 2 = sin ⁡ θ d θ d φ dOmega_(2)=sin theta d theta d varphid \Omega_{2}=\sin \theta d \theta d \varphidΩ2=sin⁡θdθdφ
It follows that
∬ ⋯ ∫ d x 1 d x 2 ⋯ d x n = ∬ ⋯ ∫ r n − 1 d r d Ω n − 1 = n C n ∫ r n − 1 d r ∬ ⋯ ∫ d x 1 d x 2 ⋯ d x n = ∬ ⋯ ∫ r n − 1 d r d Ω n − 1 = n C n ∫ r n − 1 d r {:[∬cdots intdx_(1)dx_(2)cdots dx_(n)],[=∬cdots intr^(n-1)drdOmega_(n-1)],[=nC_(n)intr^(n-1)dr]:}\begin{aligned} \iint \cdots \int & d x_{1} d x_{2} \cdots d x_{n} \\ & =\iint \cdots \int r^{n-1} d r d \Omega_{n-1} \\ & =n C_{n} \int r^{n-1} d r \end{aligned}∬⋯∫dx1dx2⋯dxn=∬⋯∫rn−1drdΩn−1=nCn∫rn−1dr
thus
n C n = ∫ S ⋯ S d Ω n − 1 n C n = ∫ S ⋯ S d Ω n − 1 nC_(n)=int S cdots SdOmega_(n-1)n C_{n}=\int S \cdots S d \Omega_{n-1}nCn=∫S⋯SdΩn−1
This integral can be evaluated by considering the integral of the function
e − ( x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 ) = e − r 2 e − x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 = e − r 2 e^(-(x_(1)^(2)+x_(2)^(2)+cdots+x_(n)^(2)))=e^(-r^(2))e^{-\left(x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+\cdots+x_{n}^{2}\right)}=e^{-r^{2}}e−(x12+x22+⋯+xn2)=e−r2
Now
∬ − ∞ ∞ ⋯ ∫ e − ( x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 ) d x 1 d x 2 ⋯ d x n ∬ − ∞ ∞   ⋯ ∫ e − x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 d x 1 d x 2 ⋯ d x n ∬_(-oo)^(oo)cdots inte^(-(x_(1)^(2)+x_(2)^(2)+cdots+x_(n)^(2)))dx_(1)dx_(2)cdots dx_(n)\iint_{-\infty}^{\infty} \cdots \int e^{-\left(x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+\cdots+x_{n}^{2}\right)} d x_{1} d x_{2} \cdots d x_{n}∬−∞∞⋯∫e−(x12+x22+⋯+xn2)dx1dx2⋯dxn
= ∫ 0 ∞ r n − 1 e − r 2 d r ∬ ⋯ ∫ d Ω n − 1 = ∫ 0 ∞   r n − 1 e − r 2 d r ∬ ⋯ ∫ d Ω n − 1 =int_(0)^(oo)r^(n-1)e^(-r^(2))dr∬cdots int dOmega_(n-1)=\int_{0}^{\infty} r^{n-1} e^{-r^{2}} d r \iint \cdots \int d \Omega_{n-1}=∫0∞rn−1e−r2dr∬⋯∫dΩn−1
Now the righthand side can be written as a product of n n nnn identical integrals
∬ − ∞ ∞ ⋯ ∫ e − ( x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 ) d x 1 d x 1 ⋯ d x n ∬ − ∞ ∞   ⋯ ∫ e − x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 d x 1 d x 1 ⋯ d x n ∬_(-oo)^(oo)cdots inte^(-(x_(1)^(2)+x_(2)^(2)+cdots+x_(n)^(2)))dx_(1)dx_(1)cdots dx_(n)\iint_{-\infty}^{\infty} \cdots \int e^{-\left(x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+\cdots+x_{n}^{2}\right)} d x_{1} d x_{1} \cdots d x_{n}∬−∞∞⋯∫e−(x12+x22+⋯+xn2)dx1dx1⋯dxn
= ∫ − ∞ ∞ e − x 1 2 d x 1 ∫ − ∞ ∞ e − x 2 2 d x 2 ⋯ ∫ − ∞ ∞ e − x n 2 d x n = ∫ − ∞ ∞   e − x 1 2 d x 1 ∫ − ∞ ∞   e − x 2 2 d x 2 ⋯ ∫ − ∞ ∞   e − x n 2 d x n =int_(-oo)^(oo)e^(-x_(1)^(2))dx_(1)int_(-oo)^(oo)e^(-x_(2)^(2))dx_(2)cdotsint_(-oo)^(oo)e^(-x_(n)^(2))dx_(n)=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x_{1}^{2}} d x_{1} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x_{2}^{2}} d x_{2} \cdots \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x_{n}^{2}} d x_{n}=∫−∞∞e−x12dx1∫−∞∞e−x22dx2⋯∫−∞∞e−xn2dxn
but
∫ − ∞ ∞ e − x 2 d x = π ∫ − ∞ ∞   e − x 2 d x = π int_(-oo)^(oo)e^(-x^(2))dx=sqrtpi\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x^{2}} d x=\sqrt{\pi}∫−∞∞e−x2dx=π
thus
∬ − ∞ ∞ ⋯ ∫ e − ( x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 ) d x 1 d x 2 ⋯ d x n = π n / 2 ∬ − ∞ ∞   ⋯ ∫ e − x 1 2 + x 2 2 + ⋯ + x n 2 d x 1 d x 2 ⋯ d x n = π n / 2 {:[∬_(-oo)^(oo)cdots inte^(-(x_(1)^(2)+x_(2)^(2)+cdots+x_(n)^(2)))dx_(1)dx_(2)cdots dx_(n)],[=pi^(n//2)]:}\begin{aligned} & \iint_{-\infty}^{\infty} \cdots \int e^{-\left(x_{1}^{2}+x_{2}^{2}+\cdots+x_{n}^{2}\right)} d x_{1} d x_{2} \cdots d x_{n} \\ & =\pi^{n / 2} \end{aligned}∬−∞∞⋯∫e−(x12+x22+⋯+xn2)dx1dx2⋯dxn=πn/2
For the lefthard side the integral over the angular coordinates
Can be evaluated since the integrand depends only on r r rrr. using
n C n = S S ⋯ S d Ω n − 1 n C n = S S ⋯ S d Ω n − 1 nC_(n)=SS cdots SdOmega_(n-1)n C_{n}=S S \cdots S d \Omega_{n-1}nCn=SS⋯SdΩn−1
gives
∫ 0 ∞ r n − 1 e − r 2 d r ∬ … ∫ d Ω n − 1 = n C n ∫ 0 ∞ r n − 1 e − r 2 d r ∫ 0 ∞   r n − 1 e − r 2 d r ∬ … ∫ d Ω n − 1 = n C n ∫ 0 ∞   r n − 1 e − r 2 d r {:[int_(0)^(oo)r^(n-1)e^(-r^(2))dr∬dots int dOmega_(n-1)],[=nCnint_(0)^(oo)r^(n-1)e^(-r^(2))dr]:}\begin{aligned} & \int_{0}^{\infty} r^{n-1} e^{-r^{2}} d r \iint \ldots \int d \Omega_{n-1} \\ = & n C n \int_{0}^{\infty} r^{n-1} e^{-r^{2}} d r \end{aligned}∫0∞rn−1e−r2dr∬…∫dΩn−1=nCn∫0∞rn−1e−r2dr
The function Γ ( n ) Γ ( n ) Gamma(n)\Gamma(n)Γ(n) is defined by
Γ ( n ) = ∫ 0 ∞ x n − 1 e − x d x Γ ( n ) = ∫ 0 ∞   x n − 1 e − x d x Gamma(n)=int_(0)^(oo)x^(n-1)e^(-x)dx\Gamma(n)=\int_{0}^{\infty} x^{n-1} e^{-x} d xΓ(n)=∫0∞xn−1e−xdx
making the change of variables
x = r 2 x = r 2 x=r^(2)x=r^{2}x=r2
so
r n − 2 = r 2 ( n 2 − 1 ) = ( r 2 ) ( n 2 − 1 ) = x ( n 2 − 1 ) r n − 2 = r 2 n 2 − 1 = r 2 n 2 − 1 = x n 2 − 1 {:[r^(n-2)=r^(2((n)/(2)-1))],[=(r^(2))^(((n)/(2)-1))],[=x^(((n)/(2)-1))]:}\begin{aligned} r^{n-2} & =r^{2\left(\frac{n}{2}-1\right)} \\ & =\left(r^{2}\right)^{\left(\frac{n}{2}-1\right)} \\ & =x^{\left(\frac{n}{2}-1\right)} \end{aligned}rn−2=r2(n2−1)=(r2)(n2−1)=x(n2−1)
and
d x = 2 r d r ⇒ 1 2 d x = r d r d x = 2 r d r ⇒ 1 2 d x = r d r dx=2rdr=>(1)/(2)dx=rdrd x=2 r d r \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} d x=r d rdx=2rdr⇒12dx=rdr
Bringing things logether gives
∫ 0 ∞ r n − 1 e − r 2 d r = ∫ 0 ∞ r n − 2 e − r 2 r d r = 1 2 ∫ 0 ∞ x ( n 2 − 1 ) e − x d x = 1 2 Γ ( n 2 ) ∫ 0 ∞   r n − 1 e − r 2 d r = ∫ 0 ∞   r n − 2 e − r 2 r d r = 1 2 ∫ 0 ∞   x n 2 − 1 e − x d x = 1 2 Γ n 2 {:[int_(0)^(oo)r^(n-1)e^(-r^(2))dr=int_(0)^(oo)r^(n-2)e^(-r^(2))rdr],[=(1)/(2)int_(0)^(oo)x^(((n)/(2)-1))e^(-x)dx],[=(1)/(2)Gamma((n)/(2))]:}\begin{aligned} \int_{0}^{\infty} r^{n-1} e^{-r^{2}} d r & =\int_{0}^{\infty} r^{n-2} e^{-r^{2}} r d r \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\infty} x^{\left(\frac{n}{2}-1\right)} e^{-x} d x \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) \end{aligned}∫0∞rn−1e−r2dr=∫0∞rn−2e−r2rdr=12∫0∞x(n2−1)e−xdx=12Γ(n2)
This expression can be simplified further by the following property of eamma functions
Γ ( n + 1 ) = ∫ 0 ∞ x n e − x d x = ∫ 0 ∞ x x n − 1 e − x d x Γ ( n + 1 ) = ∫ 0 ∞   x n e − x d x = ∫ 0 ∞   x x n − 1 e − x d x {:[Gamma(n+1)=int_(0)^(oo)x^(n)e^(-x)dx],[=int_(0)^(oo)xx^(n-1)e^(-x)dx]:}\begin{aligned} \Gamma(n+1) & =\int_{0}^{\infty} x^{n} e^{-x} d x \\ & =\int_{0}^{\infty} x x^{n-1} e^{-x} d x \end{aligned}Γ(n+1)=∫0∞xne−xdx=∫0∞xxn−1e−xdx
This integral can be evaluated using integration by parts. Let
u = x n ⇒ d u = n x n − 1 d x u = x n ⇒ d u = n x n − 1 d x u=x^(n)quad=>quad du=nx^(n-1)dxu=x^{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad d u=n x^{n-1} d xu=xn⇒du=nxn−1dx
d v = e − x d x ⇒ v = − e − x d v = e − x d x ⇒ v = − e − x dv=e^(-x)dx=>v=-e^(-x)d v=e^{-x} d x \Rightarrow v=-e^{-x}dv=e−xdx⇒v=−e−x
so
Γ ( n + 1 ) = − x n e − x | 0 ∞ + ∫ 0 ∞ n x n − 1 e − x d x = n Γ ( n ) Γ ( n + 1 ) = − x n e − x 0 ∞ + ∫ 0 ∞   n x n − 1 e − x d x = n Γ ( n ) {:[Gamma(n+1)=-x^(n)e^(-x)|_(0)^(oo)+int_(0)^(oo)nx^(n-1)e^(-x)dx],[=n Gamma(n)]:}\begin{aligned} \Gamma(n+1) & =-\left.x^{n} e^{-x}\right|_{0} ^{\infty}+\int_{0}^{\infty} n x^{n-1} e^{-x} d x \\ & =n \Gamma(n) \end{aligned}Γ(n+1)=−xne−x|0∞+∫0∞nxn−1e−xdx=nΓ(n)
using this identity/gives
Γ ( n 2 + 1 ) = n 2 Γ ( n 2 ) Γ n 2 + 1 = n 2 Γ n 2 Gamma((n)/(2)+1)=(n)/(2)Gamma((n)/(2))\Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)=\frac{n}{2} \Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2}\right)Γ(n2+1)=n2Γ(n2)
An expressish for C n C n C_(n)C_{n}Cn can now be determined
Bringing things together
π n / 2 = n C n ∫ 0 ∞ r n − 1 e − r 2 d r = n C n 1 2 Γ ( n 2 ) = C n Γ ( n 2 + 1 ) ⇒ C n = π n / 2 Γ ( n 2 + 1 ) π n / 2 = n C n ∫ 0 ∞   r n − 1 e − r 2 d r = n C n 1 2 Γ n 2 = C n Γ n 2 + 1 ⇒ C n = π n / 2 Γ n 2 + 1 {:[pi^(n//2)=nC_(n)int_(0)^(oo)r^(n-1)e^(-r^(2))dr],[=nC_(n)(1)/(2)Gamma((n)/(2))],[=C_(n)Gamma((n)/(2)+1)],[=>Cn=(pi^(n//2))/(Gamma((n)/(2)+1))]:}\begin{aligned} \pi^{n / 2} & =n C_{n} \int_{0}^{\infty} r^{n-1} e^{-r^{2}} d r \\ & =n C_{n} \frac{1}{2} \Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) \\ & =C_{n} \Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right) \\ \Rightarrow C n & =\frac{\pi^{n / 2}}{\Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)} \end{aligned}πn/2=nCn∫0∞rn−1e−r2dr=nCn12Γ(n2)=CnΓ(n2+1)⇒Cn=πn/2Γ(n2+1)
and finally
V n ( R ) = π n / 2 Γ ( n 2 + 1 ) R n V n ( R ) = π n / 2 Γ n 2 + 1 R n V_(n)(R)=(pi^(n//2))/(Gamma((n)/(2)+1))R^(n)V_{n}(R)=\frac{\pi^{n / 2}}{\Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)} R^{n}Vn(R)=πn/2Γ(n2+1)Rn
and
S n − 1 ( R ) = d V n ( R ) d R = n π n / 2 Γ ( n 2 + 1 ) R n − 1 S n − 1 ( R ) = d V n ( R ) d R = n π n / 2 Γ n 2 + 1 R n − 1 S_(n-1)(R)=(dV_(n)(R))/(dR)=(npi^(n//2))/(Gamma((n)/(2)+1))R^(n-1)S_{n-1}(R)=\frac{d V_{n}(R)}{d R}=\frac{n \pi^{n / 2}}{\Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)} R^{n-1}Sn−1(R)=dVn(R)dR=nπn/2Γ(n2+1)Rn−1
So
V n ( R ) S n − 1 ( R ) = R n V n ( R ) S n − 1 ( R ) = R n (V_(n)(R))/(S_(n-1)(R))=(R)/(n)\frac{V_{n}(R)}{S_{n-1}(R)}=\frac{R}{n}Vn(R)Sn−1(R)=Rn
To verify for n = 2 n = 2 n=2n=2n=2
V 2 ( R ) = π Γ ( 2 ) R 2 = π R 2 V 2 ( R ) = π Γ ( 2 ) R 2 = π R 2 V_(2)(R)=(pi)/(Gamma(2))R^(2)=piR^(2)V_{2}(R)=\frac{\pi}{\Gamma(2)} R^{2}=\pi R^{2}V2(R)=πΓ(2)R2=πR2
and for n = 3 n = 3 n=3n=3n=3
Γ ( 5 2 ) = 3 2 Γ ( 3 2 ) = ( 3 2 ) ( 1 2 ) Γ ( 1 2 ) Γ 5 2 = 3 2 Γ 3 2 = 3 2 1 2 Γ 1 2 Gamma((5)/(2))=(3)/(2)Gamma((3)/(2))=((3)/(2))((1)/(2))Gamma((1)/(2))\Gamma\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)=\frac{3}{2} \Gamma\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \Gamma\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)Γ(52)=32Γ(32)=(32)(12)Γ(12)
Now
Γ ( 1 2 ) = ∫ 0 ∞ x − 1 / 2 e − x d x Γ 1 2 = ∫ 0 ∞   x − 1 / 2 e − x d x Gamma((1)/(2))=int_(0)^(oo)x^(-1//2)e^(-x)dx\Gamma\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\int_{0}^{\infty} x^{-1 / 2} e^{-x} d xΓ(12)=∫0∞x−1/2e−xdx
making the change of variables
x = y 2 ⇒ d x = 2 y d y x = y 2 ⇒ d x = 2 y d y x=y^(2)=>dx=2ydyx=y^{2} \Rightarrow d x=2 y d yx=y2⇒dx=2ydy
80
Γ ( 1 2 ) = ∫ 0 ∞ y − 1 e − y 2 ( 2 y ) d y 2 ∫ 0 ∞ e − y 2 d y = 2 ( π 2 ) = π Γ 1 2 = ∫ 0 ∞   y − 1 e − y 2 ( 2 y ) d y 2 ∫ 0 ∞   e − y 2 d y = 2 π 2 = π {:[Gamma((1)/(2))=int_(0)^(oo)y^(-1)e^(-y^(2))(2y)dy],[2int_(0)^(oo)e^(-y^(2))dy=2((sqrtpi)/(2))],[=sqrtpi]:}\begin{aligned} & \Gamma\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\int_{0}^{\infty} y^{-1} e^{-y^{2}}(2 y) d y \\ & 2 \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-y^{2}} d y=2\left(\frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}\right) \\ &=\sqrt{\pi} \end{aligned}Γ(12)=∫0∞y−1e−y2(2y)dy2∫0∞e−y2dy=2(π2)=π
It follows that
Γ ( 5 2 ) = ( 3 2 ) ( 1 2 ) π = 3 4 π Γ 5 2 = 3 2 1 2 π = 3 4 π Gamma((5)/(2))=((3)/(2))((1)/(2))sqrtpi=(3)/(4)sqrtpi\Gamma\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)=\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) \sqrt{\pi}=\frac{3}{4} \sqrt{\pi}Γ(52)=(32)(12)π=34π
so the volume of a 3 sphere is given by
V 3 ( R ) = π 3 / 2 Γ ( 5 2 ) R 3 = π 3 / 2 3 4 π R 3 = 4 3 π R 3 V 3 ( R ) = π 3 / 2 Γ 5 2 R 3 = π 3 / 2 3 4 π R 3 = 4 3 π R 3 {:[V_(3)(R)=(pi^(3//2))/(Gamma((5)/(2)))R^(3)],[=(pi^(3//2))/((3)/(4)sqrtpi)R^(3)],[=(4)/(3)piR^(3)]:}\begin{aligned} V_{3}(R) & =\frac{\pi^{3 / 2}}{\Gamma\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)} R^{3} \\ & =\frac{\pi^{3 / 2}}{\frac{3}{4} \sqrt{\pi}} R^{3} \\ & =\frac{4}{3} \pi R^{3} \end{aligned}V3(R)=π3/2Γ(52)R3=π3/234πR3=43πR3
It is teresting to consider a few limits Recall
V n ( R ) = π n / 2 Γ ( n 2 + 1 ) R n V n ( R ) = π n / 2 Γ n 2 + 1 R n V_(n)(R)=(pi^(n//2))/(Gamma((n)/(2)+1))R^(n)V_{n}(R)=\frac{\pi^{n / 2}}{\Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)} R^{n}Vn(R)=πn/2Γ(n2+1)Rn
Now for n ≫ 1 n ≫ 1 n≫1n \gg 1n≫1 sterling's approximation gives
Γ ( n + 1 ) = 2 π n ( n e ) n Γ ( n + 1 ) = 2 π n n e n Gamma(n+1)=sqrt(2pi n)((n)/(e))^(n)\Gamma(n+1)=\sqrt{2 \pi n}\left(\frac{n}{e}\right)^{n}Γ(n+1)=2πn(ne)n
So
Γ ( n 2 + 1 ) ≈ π n ( n 2 e ) n 2 Γ n 2 + 1 ≈ π n n 2 e n 2 Gamma((n)/(2)+1)~~sqrt(pi n)((n)/(2e))^((n)/(2))\Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right) \approx \sqrt{\pi n}\left(\frac{n}{2 e}\right)^{\frac{n}{2}}Γ(n2+1)≈πn(n2e)n2
Now
V n ( R ) = π n 2 Γ ( n 2 + 1 ) R n = π n 2 R n [ π n ( n 2 e ) n 2 ] − 1 = π n 2 R n [ 1 π n ( n 2 e ) − n 2 ] = π n 2 π R n 1 n ( n 2 e ) − n 2 = π n 2 π R n 1 n ( 2 e n ) n 2 = 1 n π ( 2 π e n ) n 2 R n = 1 n π ( 2 π e R 2 n ) n 2 V n ( R ) = π n 2 Γ n 2 + 1 R n = π n 2 R n π n n 2 e n 2 − 1 = π n 2 R n 1 π n n 2 e − n 2 = π n 2 π R n 1 n n 2 e − n 2 = π n 2 π R n 1 n 2 e n n 2 = 1 n π 2 π e n n 2 R n = 1 n π 2 π e R 2 n n 2 {:[V_(n)(R)=(pi^((n)/(2)))/(Gamma((n)/(2)+1))R^(n)],[=pi^((n)/(2))R^(n)[sqrt(pi n)((n)/(2e))^((n)/(2))]^(-1)],[=pi^((n)/(2))R^(n)[(1)/(sqrt(pi n))((n)/(2e))^(-(n)/(2))]],[=(pi^((n)/(2)))/(sqrtpi)R^(n)(1)/(sqrtn)((n)/(2e))^(-(n)/(2))],[=(pi^((n)/(2)))/(sqrtpi)R^(n)(1)/(sqrtn)((2e)/(n))^((n)/(2))],[=(1)/(sqrt(n pi))((2pi e)/(n))^((n)/(2))R^(n)],[=(1)/(sqrt(n pi))((2pi eR^(2))/(n))^((n)/(2))]:}\begin{aligned} V_{n}(R) & =\frac{\pi^{\frac{n}{2}}}{\Gamma\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)} R^{n} \\ & =\pi^{\frac{n}{2}} R^{n}\left[\sqrt{\pi n}\left(\frac{n}{2 e}\right)^{\frac{n}{2}}\right]^{-1} \\ & =\pi^{\frac{n}{2}} R^{n}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi n}}\left(\frac{n}{2 e}\right)^{-\frac{n}{2}}\right] \\ & =\frac{\pi^{\frac{n}{2}}}{\sqrt{\pi}} R^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\left(\frac{n}{2 e}\right)^{-\frac{n}{2}} \\ & =\frac{\pi^{\frac{n}{2}}}{\sqrt{\pi}} R^{n} \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}}\left(\frac{2 e}{n}\right)^{\frac{n}{2}} \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{n \pi}}\left(\frac{2 \pi e}{n}\right)^{\frac{n}{2}} R^{n} \\ & =\frac{1}{\sqrt{n \pi}}\left(\frac{2 \pi e R^{2}}{n}\right)^{\frac{n}{2}} \end{aligned}Vn(R)=πn2Γ(n2+1)Rn=πn2Rn[πn(n2e)n2]−1=πn2Rn[1πn(n2e)−n2]=πn2πRn1n(n2e)−n2=πn2πRn1n(2en)n2=1nπ(2πen)n2Rn=1nπ(2πeR2n)n2
Thus the volume of an n n nnn-dimensional sphere is given by
V n ( R ) = 1 n π ( 2 π e R 2 n ) n 2 V n ( R ) = 1 n π 2 π e R 2 n n 2 V_(n)(R)=(1)/(sqrt(n pi))((2pi eR^(2))/(n))^((n)/(2))V_{n}(R)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{n \pi}}\left(\frac{2 \pi e R^{2}}{n}\right)^{\frac{n}{2}}Vn(R)=1nπ(2πeR2n)n2
Consider the limit n → ∞ n → ∞ n rarr oon \rightarrow \inftyn→∞ with R R RRR fixed. Recall that
lim n → ∞ ( A n ) n B = 0 lim n → ∞   A n n B = 0 lim_(n rarr oo)((A)/(n))^((n)/(B))=0\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left(\frac{A}{n}\right)^{\frac{n}{B}}=0limn→∞(An)nB=0
it follows that for a fixed radius R R RRR the volume of a hypersphere vanishes as the dimension of the space goes to infinity,
lim n → ∞ V n ( R ) = lim n → ∞ 1 n π ( 2 π e R 2 n ) n 2 = 0 lim n → ∞   V n ( R ) = lim n → ∞   1 n π 2 π e R 2 n n 2 = 0 {:[lim_(n rarr oo)V_(n)(R)=lim_(n rarr oo)(1)/(sqrt(n pi))((2pi eR^(2))/(n))^((n)/(2))],[=0]:}\begin{aligned} \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} V_{n}(R) & =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{\sqrt{n \pi}}\left(\frac{2 \pi e R^{2}}{n}\right)^{\frac{n}{2}} \\ & =0 \end{aligned}limn→∞Vn(R)=limn→∞1nπ(2πeR2n)n2=0
